For two days and nights, he floated about, drifting hither and thither by the angry billows, until at last, after many a narrow escape from death, the sea-goddess Leucothea came to his aid, and he was cast ashore on the coast of Scheria, the island of Phaeaces. They bathed and then sat down to eat. Afterward, they entertained themselves by singing and playing a game of ball. After hearing from him the account of his shipwreck and the terrible hardships he had endured, Nausicaa called back her attendants, reproached them for their lack of courtesy, and bade them supply the wanderer with food, drink, and suitable clothing.

When the hero heard that this was indeed his native land, which, after an absence of twenty years, the gods had finally allowed him to see again, he fell to the ground and kissed it with great joy. The goddess, who had meanwhile revealed her identity to Odysseus, now helped him to hide the valuable gifts of the Phaeacian king in a nearby cave. After the banquet was over, Penelope took down the great bow of Odysseus from its place and entered the hall. She declared that whoever among her suitors could bend it and send an arrow through twelve rings—a feat she had often seen Odysseus perform—would be chosen by her as her husband.

He and his father now attacked the riotous revellers, and after a desperate encounter, not one of the whole crew remained alive. She therefore commanded in his hearing that his own bed be brought from his chamber. Knowing this, he exclaimed that the errand was useless, for no mortal could stir it from its place.

The struggle, however, was brief. After a short contest, negotiations of a peaceful nature were entered into between Odysseus and his subjects. Recognizing the justice of his cause, they became reconciled to their chief, who would reign over them for many years.

This ancient writer left behind two great poems: "The Works and Days," which contains some of the earliest Greek legends, and "The Theogony," which outlines the genealogies of the gods. Unfortunately, both poems have been heavily interpolated by the writers of the Alexandrian school, rendering them unreliable sources for understanding the early beliefs of the Greek nation.
